sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Synchronised cardioversion delivers an electrical shock timed to the R-wave of the QRS complex to avoid delivery during the relative refractory period (T-wave), which could induce VF. It is used for haemodynamically unstable tachyarrhythmias with a pulse.

sections.title: Indications & Contraindications
sections.content:
**Indications (haemodynamically unstable with pulse):**
- Atrial fibrillation or flutter
- SVT refractory to simpler measures where the patient is unstable
- VT with pulse

**Contraindications:**
- Pulseless rhythm
- Digoxin toxicity is a relative concern
- Multifocal atrial tachycardia will not reliably cardiovert

**Signs of haemodynamic instability:** altered conscious state, ischaemic chest pain, acute heart failure, hypotension, or shock

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Confirm haemodynamic instability - if stable, pharmacological cardioversion preferred

2. Sedate if time permits and patient is conscious - midazolam ± ketamine per protocol

3. Apply pads (same positions as defibrillation)

4. Confirm synchronise mode is active (should see sync marker on R-waves)

5. Energy selection:
   - AF/flutter: 100–200 J biphasic
   - SVT / regular narrow complex: 50–100 J
   - VT with pulse: 100–200 J

6. Charge; confirm all clear

7. Deliver shock (device may delay briefly to synchronise with R-wave)

8. Reassess rhythm and haemodynamic status

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- After each shock the monitor may revert to unsynchronised mode, so reactivate sync before every subsequent shock
- Document pre- and post-shock rhythm, energy used, number of shocks, sedation given, and patient response
- If the patient loses pulse, switch immediately to the cardiac arrest sequence and unsynchronised defibrillation where appropriate
- Do not delay treatment of true instability because of longer-term anticoagulation considerations that are hospital decisions
